message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:p>
                  A novel coronavirus (SARS-CoV-2) has recently emerged as a global threat. As the epidemic progresses, many disease modelers have focused on estimating the basic reproductive number
                  <jats:italic>ℛ</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>0</jats:sub>
                  – the average number of secondary cases caused by a primary case in an otherwise susceptible population. The modeling approaches and resulting estimates of
                  <jats:italic>ℛ</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>0</jats:sub>
                  vary widely, despite relying on similar data sources. Here, we present a novel statistical framework for comparing and combining different estimates of
                  <jats:italic>ℛ</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>0</jats:sub>
                  across a wide range of models by decomposing the basic reproductive number into three key quantities: the exponential growth rate
                  <jats:italic>r</jats:italic>
                  , the mean generation interval
                  <jats:inline-formula>
                    <jats:alternatives>
                      <jats:inline-graphic xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink" xlink:href="20019877v4_inline1.gif"/>
                    </jats:alternatives>
                  </jats:inline-formula>
                  , and the generation-interval dispersion
                  <jats:italic>κ</jats:italic>
                  . We then apply our framework to early estimates of
                  <jats:italic>ℛ</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>0</jats:sub>
                  for the SARS-CoV-2 outbreak. We show that many early
                  <jats:italic>ℛ</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>0</jats:sub>
                  estimates are overly confident. Our results emphasize the importance of propagating uncertainties in all components of
                  <jats:italic>ℛ</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>0</jats:sub>
                  , including the shape of the generation-interval distribution, in efforts to estimate
                  <jats:italic>ℛ</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>0</jats:sub>
                  at the outset of an epidemic.
                </jats:p>
title: Reconciling early-outbreak estimates of the basic reproductive number and its uncertainty: framework and applications to the novel coronavirus (SARS-CoV-2) outbreak

reference.unstructured: Anderson, R. M. and R. M. May (1991). Infectious diseases of humans: dynamics and control. Oxford university press.

Bedford, T. , R. Neher , J. Hadfield , E. Hodcroft , M. Ilcisin , and N. Müller (2020). Genomic analysis of nCoV spread. Situation report 2020-01-23. https://nextstrain.org/narratives/ncov/sit-rep/2020-01-23. Accessed 24, January, 2020.

reference.article-title: Estimation in emerging epidemics: Biases and remedies

reference.unstructured: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (2020). 2019 Novel Coronavirus (2019-nCoV), Wuhan, China. https://www.cdc.gov/coronavirus/2019-ncov/summary.html. Accessed 29, January, 2020.
Fisman, D. N. , T. S. Hauck , A. R. Tuite , and A. L. Greer (2013). An IDEA for short term outbreak projection: nearcasting using the basic reproduction number. PloS One 8 (12).

reference.article-title: Prior distributions for variance parameters in hierarchical models (comment on article by Browne and Draper)

reference.article-title: A practical generation-interval-based approach to inferring the strength of epidemics from their speed

reference.article-title: Assessing reporting delays and the effective reproduction number: The Ebola epidemic in DRC, May 2018–January 2019
